DOM shows historical average signal levels from the past 30 days; it cannot display real-time signal strength and requires a 30-day collection period before providing any data

explanations.a: DOM as real-time transceiver parameter monitoring via I2C, the specific parameters (TX power, RX power, bias current, temperature, voltage), path loss calculation from TX-RX difference, and threshold alerting capability are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: DOM measures optical physical layer parameters (power, temperature, voltage, laser current) not network traffic metrics.
explanations.c: DOM is defined in the SFF-8472 standard and is implemented by many third-party transceiver vendors; it is not exclusively proprietary though some vendors require configuration to accept third-party transceivers.
explanations.d: DOM provides real-time instantaneous readings not historical averages; there is no 30-day collection period.

questions.text: DOM output shows: TX Power = -3.5 dBm (normal range -3 to -9 dBm), RX Power = -18 dBm (normal range -3 to -14 dBm). The link is up but experiencing intermittent packet loss. How should this data be interpreted and what does -18 dBm vs the -14 dBm minimum suggest?

answers.text: The DOM data shows no issues; -18 dBm receive power is within normal operation because it is close to the -14 dBm minimum and links can tolerate being slightly below specification
The link should be operating perfectly; intermittent packet loss with these DOM values is caused by the network protocol not the physical layer; investigate TCP or BGP configuration instead

The DOM data reveals a marginal receive signal condition: TX power at -3.5 dBm is within specification confirming the local transmitter is functioning correctly; RX power at -18 dBm is below the stated normal range minimum of -14 dBm meaning the received optical power is 4 dBm below the acceptable minimum; while the transceiver has a minimum sensitivity threshold below which it cannot detect signal at all (-14 dBm is the specified normal range minimum not necessarily the absolute sensitivity cutoff) operating outside the normal range indicates insufficient power margin; the marginal power causes the receiver to struggle with signal detection during any additional loss events (temperature changes affecting connector expansion, micro-vibrations causing momentary connector seating variation, temperature affecting fiber attenuation slightly) which explains the intermittent packet loss — normally the receiver can decode the marginal signal but brief additional loss events push the signal below the decode threshold; path loss calculation: -3.5 dBm TX minus -18 dBm RX = 14.5 dB total path loss; for the expected fiber run this should be 3-8 dB indicating 6-11 dB of excess loss; investigation: clean all fiber connectors on this link using proper cleaning tools and re-measure; if power improves the excess loss was from contamination; if power does not improve after cleaning use an OTDR to locate the specific high-loss point (damaged connector, bend, splice)
Replace both transceivers immediately; RX power below -14 dBm always indicates transceiver hardware failure requiring replacement before the link completely fails

explanations.a: Operating below the specified normal range minimum with a 4 dBm deficit is a significant concern; intermittent packet loss from marginal power is a real and documented failure mode.
explanations.b: Marginal optical power causing intermittent packet loss is a physical layer issue; the DOM data directly supports this diagnosis.
explanations.c: TX within spec confirming local transmitter health, RX below normal range revealing marginal receive power, 14.5 dB path loss calculation revealing excess loss, explanation of intermittent loss from marginal margin, and connector cleaning followed by OTDR as the investigation steps are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Low RX power typically indicates fiber path loss (dirty connectors, damage) rather than transceiver failure; cleaning and OTDR investigation should precede transceiver replacement.

questions.text: A network engineer establishes baseline DOM readings for all fiber links in a data center. Six months later, monitoring shows TX power has decreased by 3 dBm on 15 transceivers spread across different switches while RX power at the far end decreased proportionally. All affected transceivers are from the same vendor batch. What does this pattern indicate?

answers.text: TX power decreasing by 3 dBm across 15 transceivers simultaneously is normal aging; all optical transceivers lose exactly 3 dBm of TX power after six months of operation

The 3 dBm TX power decrease means the transceivers are overperforming; transceivers self-regulate to reduce TX power when they detect the fiber path has low loss; this is normal adaptive power control

TX power decreases when data traffic increases; the 15 transceivers are on the busiest links in the data center and their high traffic load is consuming laser power; reduce traffic on those links to restore TX power

Simultaneous TX power decrease across 15 transceivers from the same vendor batch indicates a batch quality issue or shared environmental factor: same vendor batch — the transceivers may have had a manufacturing defect (laser quality, laser drive circuit, or thermal management issue) that causes premature laser output degradation; the simultaneous nature across 15 units from the same batch strongly suggests a batch defect rather than independent random failures; laser aging is normal but should be gradual and individualized not simultaneous across a batch; shared environmental factor — if all 15 are in switches in the same room or rack experiencing elevated temperature the thermal stress could accelerate laser aging across all units simultaneously; 3 dBm decrease in TX power is significant — if original TX was -3 dBm the new TX is -6 dBm and RX at the far end decreased proportionally; if the receive end is already near its minimum sensitivity threshold a further 3 dBm reduction may push links into failure; actions: contact the transceiver vendor and document the batch serial numbers and failure data; request RMA (Return Merchandise Authorization) for the affected units as a batch defect may be covered under warranty; verify data center temperature in the affected areas; proactively monitor all remaining transceivers from the same batch for similar degradation; replace high-priority links' transceivers before they fail completely rather than waiting for link failure; establish monitoring thresholds (warnings at 2 dBm below baseline) to detect further degradation before links fail

explanations.a: 3 dBm decrease in 6 months is faster than normal laser aging and simultaneous occurrence across a batch is not the expected pattern for random aging.
explanations.b: Standard commercial transceivers do not have automatic TX power reduction based on detected fiber loss; TX power is set by the laser bias current which is fixed (or slowly aging) not dynamically reduced.
explanations.c: Laser TX power is not modulated by data traffic volume; lasers emit at a configured bias current level independent of data content or traffic load.
explanations.d: Batch defect as the likely cause of simultaneous degradation across same-batch units, environmental factor as an alternative cause, significance of the 3 dBm decrease for near-threshold links, vendor RMA process, proactive batch monitoring, and preemptive replacement strategy are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: An engineer uses an optical time-domain reflectometer (OTDR) to investigate a fiber link with poor RX power. The OTDR trace shows a large reflective event at 47 meters followed by a high-loss non-reflective event at 112 meters and the fiber end at 215 meters. How should these OTDR events be interpreted and what actions do they indicate?

answers.text: OTDR traces cannot be interpreted without specialized vendor training; only certified fiber technicians with vendor certification can read OTDR output

OTDR trace interpretation: an OTDR launches light pulses into the fiber and measures the backscattered and reflected light over time (distance); each event on the trace represents a physical feature in the fiber: the reflective event at 47 meters — a large reflective spike indicates a mechanical connector or break with a gap (air gap) that causes strong Fresnel reflection; the 47-meter location indicates this is likely a connector or splice within the fiber run (not at the equipment end); the reflection magnitude indicates a connector with an air gap suggesting contamination or physical damage at that connection; a dirty or damaged connector reflects more light back and passes less forward (high insertion loss); the high-loss non-reflective event at 112 meters — a step-down in the OTDR trace without a corresponding reflection indicates a splice loss point, a sharp bend exceeding the minimum bend radius causing macrobend loss, or a physical crush or kink in the cable at that location; non-reflective high-loss events suggest mode coupling (bend loss) rather than an air gap; the fiber end at 215 meters — the large reflective peak at 215m confirms the fiber terminus (Fresnel reflection from the cleaved end); total fiber is 215m; actions: at 47 meters — clean the connector at that location with IEC 61300-3-35 compliant tools; if cleaning does not improve the reflectance and insertion loss the connector may need re-termination or replacement; at 112 meters — physically inspect the cable at that location for bends, kinks, or crushing damage; if the cable is routed around a tight corner add a radius protector; if physically damaged replace that cable section
The OTDR shows three events meaning the fiber has three breaks and must be completely replaced; any OTDR event regardless of type indicates a catastrophic fiber break

The reflective event at 47 meters is the fiber midpoint and is expected; the high-loss event at 112 meters indicates the fiber is 112 meters long and the 215-meter reading is a OTDR measurement error caused by back-reflection from the equipment room

explanations.a: OTDR interpretation is a learnable skill; the basic event types (reflective connectors, non-reflective loss events, end reflection) are documented in fiber optic training materials.
explanations.b: Reflective event at 47m as a connector with air gap (contamination/damage), non-reflective loss at 112m as a bend or physical damage, end reflection at 215m confirming fiber length, and specific remediation actions (clean/re-terminate the 47m connector, inspect/fix the 112m bend) are all correct OTDR interpretation. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: OTDR events are not all catastrophic breaks; connectors, splices, and bends appear as events on OTDR traces even when functioning; only complete breaks appear as events with no return signal beyond the event point.
explanations.d: The 47m event is at a mid-run location not a midpoint; the 215m reading is the fiber end confirmed by the large Fresnel reflection; OTDR distance measurements are based on speed of light in glass and are accurate.

questions.text: What causes a Layer 2 network loop and what visible symptoms indicate one is occurring?

answers.text: A Layer 2 network loop occurs when multiple physical paths exist between switches without a protocol like STP to block redundant links; when a broadcast frame (ARP request, DHCP discover) enters a loop topology it is forwarded out all ports on Switch A, arrives at Switch B on two paths simultaneously, Switch B forwards it again on all ports, it returns to Switch A, and the cycle repeats indefinitely; each pass multiplies the number of frame copies exponentially creating a broadcast storm; visible symptoms: all switch port LEDs flash rapidly and continuously; network utilization on all links reaches 100%; all connected devices lose network connectivity simultaneously; the switch CPU reaches 100% from processing the continuous flood of frames; network management access to switches is lost because management traffic cannot compete with the broadcast storm; the storm self-perpetuates and worsens until the loop is physically broken by removing a cable or until STP re-converges; broadcast storms can saturate a 1 Gbps network within milliseconds
A network loop is caused when a switch's MAC address table exceeds its capacity; when the table is full the switch creates a loop by forwarding frames back to their source
Network loops are caused by incorrect IP addressing; when two devices have the same IP address the resulting routing loop creates the observed symptoms
A Layer 2 loop only occurs when three or more switches are connected in a ring; two switches connected with two cables cannot form a loop because the switches detect the duplicate connection

explanations.a: Physical redundant paths without blocking causing exponential broadcast frame multiplication, all symptoms (LED flashing, 100% utilization, complete connectivity loss, switch CPU overload), and the self-perpetuating nature are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: CAM table overflow causes unknown unicast flooding (not a loop); a loop requires physical redundant paths without STP blocking one.
explanations.c: IP addressing issues cause routing loops at Layer 3; a Layer 2 broadcast storm is a separate phenomenon from IP routing loops.
explanations.d: Two switches connected with two cables form a loop; any two-switch connection with two physical paths creates a loop topology that STP must break by blocking one path.

questions.text: A broadcast storm is detected in a building. The network team suspects a newly connected unmanaged switch in a conference room created a loop. The storm must be stopped immediately. What is the fastest safe approach and why is simply unplugging the suspect switch potentially insufficient?

answers.text: Unplug all switches simultaneously and power everything down; a complete network restart is the only safe way to stop a broadcast storm
The fastest approach is enabling STP on all switches; STP convergence stops the broadcast storm within seconds of being enabled; no other action is required

Fastest safe approach: identify the switch port feeding the conference room area and administratively shut it down on the managed switch (interface GigabitEthernet0/X, shutdown); this breaks the loop at a controlled point without needing physical access to the conference room and stops the storm immediately; the managed switch can receive this command even during a storm if management connectivity can be established (out-of-band management via console is most reliable during a storm; in-band SSH may time out); why unplugging only the suspect switch is potentially insufficient: the loop may not be created by the suspect switch alone — it may involve multiple cables; there may be other unmanaged switches or devices creating loops elsewhere; the suspect switch may be connected to two ports on the managed infrastructure with both connections active simultaneously; the storm may have caused MAC address table corruption on managed switches that must stabilize after the loop is broken; after breaking the loop verify the storm has stopped (utilization drops, LEDs normalize) before restoring connectivity; then investigate the root cause before reconnecting any suspect equipment
Wait for the storm to resolve naturally; broadcast storms self-resolve within 5 minutes as switches age out their MAC address tables and the frames expire from the network

explanations.a: Shutting down all switches causes complete network outage; targeting the specific suspect port is faster and less disruptive.
explanations.b: STP convergence takes 30-50 seconds for classic STP; during that time the storm continues; enabling STP does not provide immediate storm cessation.
explanations.c: Targeted administrative shutdown of the suspect port via console (out-of-band during storm), insufficient reasons for only unplugging the switch (multiple cables, other loops, table corruption), and post-storm verification steps are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Broadcast storms do not self-resolve; they intensify as more frames accumulate; there is no natural expiration mechanism for frames in a loop.

questions.text: After a broadcast storm event analysis shows the root cause was PortFast enabled on an inter-switch uplink (trunk port). How does this misconfiguration enable a loop and what should PortFast be configured on instead?

answers.text: PortFast on uplinks does not create loops; PortFast only affects the time it takes for a port to start forwarding and has no relationship to loop creation

PortFast accelerates loop creation because it forces ports to transition through all STP states faster; the faster state transition means loops establish more quickly; disable PortFast on all ports to prevent loops

PortFast on uplinks creates loops by disabling STP entirely on those ports; once STP is disabled loops cannot be detected or prevented; PortFast should be enabled on uplinks only when RSTP is used instead of STP

PortFast is designed exclusively for access ports connected to single end devices (workstations, printers, phones); when a device connects to a PortFast-enabled access port the port transitions immediately to forwarding state (bypassing the STP listening and learning phases which take 30 seconds) eliminating the brief outage users experience when plugging in; this is safe on access ports because single end devices cannot create loops; when PortFast is incorrectly enabled on an inter-switch uplink (trunk port) the danger becomes apparent when a new switch connects: without PortFast the port waits 30 seconds in STP learning before forwarding during which STP discovers the new switch calculates a loop-free topology and blocks necessary ports; with PortFast the uplink transitions immediately to forwarding before STP can calculate the topology; during those critical seconds the new switch is forwarding and the loop exists without STP blocking; if the new switch is itself running STP the loop eventually resolves but the storm damages the network during convergence; if the new switch is unmanaged STP never resolves the loop; PortFast should only be configured on access ports connected to end devices never on uplinks or trunk ports; BPDU Guard should accompany PortFast to err-disable any PortFast port that receives a BPDU (indicating a switch was mistakenly connected)

explanations.a: PortFast on uplinks absolutely contributes to loop creation by allowing the port to forward before STP topology calculation completes; the 30-second window is when loops can cause storms.
explanations.b: PortFast does not accelerate loop creation through faster state transitions; it bypasses initial delay for safe access port connections; on uplinks it creates a temporary loop window before STP convergence not a persistent loop.
explanations.c: PortFast does not disable STP on the port; it only bypasses the listening and learning states for the initial connection; the port still participates in STP topology calculations.
explanations.d: PortFast design purpose (access ports, bypass STP delay for end devices), the danger on uplinks (immediate forwarding before STP convergence creates a storm window), BPDU Guard as the companion control, and the correct scope (access ports only, never uplinks) are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network uses RSTP (Rapid Spanning Tree). An engineer reports that topology change notifications (TCNs) are generating every 30 seconds causing MAC table flushes and periodic traffic disruption. The network is physically stable with no cable changes. What causes persistent RSTP TCNs on a stable physical network and how is it diagnosed?

answers.text: RSTP always generates TCNs every 30 seconds; this is the default hello timer behavior and 30-second TCNs are normal and expected with no impact

Persistent TCNs on a physically stable network indicate a logical instability rather than physical link changes: common causes in RSTP: edge port misconfiguration — a port configured as a non-edge port (no PortFast) connected to an end device; when the end device's NIC cycles (suspend, wake, NIC driver restart, DHCP renewal) the port link cycles causing RSTP to generate a TCN even though no switch topology changed; RSTP generates TCNs when any non-edge port transitions; if end device NICs or lightweight APs connected to switch ports cycle their link state every 30 seconds they generate consistent 30-second TCN cycles; a switch with an unstable backplane or failing supervisor causing internal port state transitions without external link change; a switch running STP in a compatibility mode that is less stable than native RSTP; BPDUs arriving from a device that should not be sending them causing repeated topology calculations; diagnosis: use 'show spanning-tree detail' to identify which ports are generating topology changes (topology changes count and the port generating the last change); enable STP logging (debug spanning-tree events) to capture the specific port and timing; if an end device port is causing TCNs enable PortFast/edge port on that interface; the per-port topology change counter in 'show spanning-tree detail' shows which port is the source; comparing the 30-second interval to specific device behaviors (screensaver, power save, DHCP lease renewal) can confirm the cause
Persistent TCNs in RSTP indicate STP has detected a physical loop that exists but cannot block; replace all switches with newer models that support loop detection
RSTP TCNs every 30 seconds indicate the root bridge is down and switches are re-electing a root bridge every 30 seconds; replace the root bridge switch which is failing

explanations.a: TCNs every 30 seconds are not normal expected behavior; they indicate a problem causing repeated topology change events.
explanations.b: Non-edge port connected to end devices cycling link state as the primary cause, specific diagnostic commands (show spanning-tree detail identifying the source port and topology change count), and end device behavior correlation (DHCP, power save, NIC cycles) are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: TCNs indicate topology changes not unresolvable loops; RSTP blocks loops effectively; persistent TCNs point to logical instability not a hidden physical loop.
explanations.d: Root bridge failure causes root bridge re-election which has distinct symptoms different from periodic TCNs; the described symptom points to a non-edge port with link cycling not root bridge instability.

questions.text: How does STP select the root bridge and what two values determine which switch wins the election?

answers.text: STP root bridge election uses the Bridge ID which consists of two values: Bridge Priority (a configurable value from 0 to 61440 in increments of 4096, default 32768) and MAC Address (the switch's base MAC address used as a tiebreaker); the switch with the lowest Bridge ID wins the election and becomes root bridge; selection process: all switches begin as root bridge candidates; they exchange BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Units) frames advertising their Bridge ID; when a switch receives a BPDU with a lower Bridge ID than its own it stops claiming to be root bridge and acknowledges the other switch as the superior; the process continues until one switch remains the root bridge (the one with the lowest Bridge ID); practical implication: if all switches use the default priority of 32768 the switch with the lowest MAC address becomes root bridge; this makes root bridge selection unpredictable and potentially suboptimal (a cheap access switch with a low MAC address could become root); best practice is explicitly setting lower priority on the intended root bridge (e.g., 4096 for primary root, 8192 for secondary root)
The switch with the most ports becomes the root bridge; more ports means more switching capacity making it the best choice for the central point of the spanning tree
The root bridge is selected manually by the network administrator by issuing a specific CLI command; STP does not automatically elect a root bridge without administrator intervention
The switch with the highest Bridge Priority number becomes root bridge; a higher priority indicates greater importance in the network hierarchy

explanations.a: Bridge ID as Priority + MAC Address, lowest Bridge ID wins, BPDU exchange mechanism, tiebreaker role of MAC when priorities are equal, and the practical unpredictability of default priority making lowest-MAC-address switch root are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Port count has no role in root bridge election; Bridge Priority and MAC Address determine the winner.
explanations.c: STP automatically elects a root bridge through BPDU exchange; administrator intervention is optional (to configure explicit priority) but not required.
explanations.d: Lower Bridge Priority number wins; a switch with priority 4096 beats one with 32768; higher number means lower priority in STP.

questions.text: A network has a core switch (CS1) with priority 4096 configured as root and a distribution switch (DS1) with default priority 32768. A new access switch (AS5) with priority 4096 is added for a new floor. After AS5 is connected, 'show spanning-tree vlan 10' on CS1 shows AS5 as the root bridge for VLAN 10. Why and how is this fixed?

answers.text: This cannot happen; a switch with priority 4096 configured as root cannot be displaced as root bridge by any other switch regardless of its priority
AS5 became root bridge because it has more uplink bandwidth than CS1; STP always selects the switch with the highest aggregate bandwidth as root bridge to optimize traffic paths

AS5 displaced CS1 as root because AS5 and CS1 have the same priority (4096) and AS5 has a lower MAC address than CS1; when two switches have identical Bridge Priority the tiebreaker is the MAC address — the lower MAC address wins; if AS5's base MAC address is numerically lower than CS1's MAC address AS5 wins the election despite both having priority 4096; this is why best practice recommends setting root bridge priority to the lowest possible value (0 is valid) for the designated root rather than 4096 which leaves room for accidental same-priority contenders; fix options: set CS1's priority lower than AS5's (e.g., CS1 to 0 or 4096 and AS5 to 8192); use the 'spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary' macro command on CS1 which automatically sets the priority to 24576 or lower than the current root's priority ensuring CS1 wins; set AS5 to a higher priority (e.g., 61440) appropriate for an access switch that should never be root; configure Root Guard on the ports of CS1 and DS1 that connect to access switches to prevent any switch connected through those ports from becoming root bridge; the permanent fix is explicit priority hierarchy: access switches at 61440, distribution at 28672, core at 4096 or lower with no overlap

The priority 4096 on AS5 is automatically inherited from CS1 when AS5 connects to the network; to prevent this disable priority inheritance by configuring AS5 with a unique priority before connecting it

explanations.a: Same-priority switches compete on MAC address tiebreaker; a switch with the same priority and a lower MAC address does win the election.
explanations.b: STP root bridge selection uses Bridge Priority and MAC Address; bandwidth has no role in root bridge selection.
explanations.c: Same-priority MAC tiebreaker causing AS5 to win, fix options (lower CS1 priority to 0, use root primary macro, raise AS5 priority, deploy Root Guard), and priority hierarchy best practice are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Priority is not automatically inherited from the root bridge when a switch connects; each switch retains its locally configured priority independently.

questions.text: Root Guard is enabled on access-layer uplink ports. A distribution switch (DS2) sends superior BPDUs on a port where Root Guard is enabled. What triggers Root Guard and what effect does it have on DS2's STP participation for that VLAN?

answers.text: Root Guard only blocks BPDUs from devices that are not switches; BPDUs from legitimate network switches like DS2 are always permitted regardless of Root Guard configuration
Root Guard allows the superior BPDU through but alerts the administrator via SNMP; traffic continues forwarding while the alert is being investigated
Root Guard permanently disables the port sending superior BPDUs by removing it from all VLANs permanently; recovery requires physical replacement of the connecting cable

Root Guard is triggered by any BPDU received on a Root Guard-enabled port that has a superior Bridge ID to the current root bridge; a 'superior BPDU' means a BPDU with a lower Bridge ID (lower priority or lower MAC with same priority) than the current root's Bridge ID; when DS2 sends a superior BPDU on a Root Guard-enabled port the switch receiving it immediately places that specific port in root-inconsistent state (displayed as 'BKN' or root-inconsistent in 'show spanning-tree'); a root-inconsistent port does not forward traffic and does not receive or process data frames effectively taking the port out of service for STP purposes; Root Guard does not err-disable the port (unlike BPDU Guard) — it uses the root-inconsistent state which automatically recovers when the superior BPDUs stop arriving; effect on DS2's STP participation: DS2 itself is unaffected and continues operating normally; only the specific port on the upstream switch that received DS2's superior BPDU is placed in root-inconsistent state; DS2's other connections continue normally; the root-inconsistent state persists only while superior BPDUs from DS2 continue arriving; if DS2 stops sending superior BPDUs (because its priority was corrected) the port automatically returns to normal forwarding state without administrator intervention; this behavior distinguishes Root Guard (automatic recovery) from BPDU Guard (requires manual or automatic err-disable recovery)

explanations.a: Root Guard triggers on any superior BPDU regardless of whether the source is a legitimate switch; it protects the root bridge position from any device with a lower Bridge ID.
explanations.b: Root Guard actively blocks traffic on the port by placing it in root-inconsistent state; it does not allow traffic to continue while alerting.
explanations.c: Root Guard places the port in root-inconsistent state which automatically recovers; it does not permanently disable the port or remove it from VLANs.
explanations.d: Superior BPDU triggering root-inconsistent state (not err-disabled), traffic forwarding cessation on the specific port, automatic recovery when superior BPDUs stop, DS2 continuing to operate normally with its other connections, and the Root Guard vs BPDU Guard recovery distinction are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network engineer is configuring a new campus network with one core switch, two distribution switches, and twenty access switches. How should STP priorities be configured and why?

answers.text: All switches should use the default priority of 32768; STP automatically determines the optimal root bridge based on switch capabilities when all switches use default priority

STP priorities should be explicitly configured to enforce the intended network hierarchy and ensure the root bridge is the most capable device for that role: core switch (CS1) — configure as primary root with the lowest priority; spanning-tree vlan [all VLANs] priority 4096 ensures CS1 wins the election for all VLANs; or use 'spanning-tree vlan [vlan] root primary' which sets priority lower than any current root; distribution switches (DS1, DS2) — configure as secondary root candidates with intermediate priority (spanning-tree vlan [vlan] priority 8192); if CS1 fails a distribution switch becomes root rather than an access switch; access switches (all 20) — configure with highest priority (spanning-tree vlan [vlan] priority 61440) ensuring they can never become root bridge; Root Guard on distribution switch ports connecting to access layer prevents access switches from sending superior BPDUs; rationale: without explicit priority the access switch with the lowest MAC address becomes root by default; access switches typically have fewer uplinks and less bandwidth making them poor root bridge candidates; all traffic in a spanning tree topology flows toward and from the root bridge so placing root at the core minimizes traffic path length; PVST+ (Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus) allows different root bridges per VLAN enabling load balancing with CS1 as root for half the VLANs and DS1 as root for the other half
Only the core switch needs explicit priority configuration; all other switches should use default priority because STP automatically determines the best topology from any root bridge
Higher priority numbers should be assigned to more important switches; the core switch should have priority 61440 and access switches should have priority 4096

explanations.a: Default priority on all switches makes root bridge selection unpredictable; the access switch with the lowest MAC address could become root creating suboptimal traffic paths.
explanations.b: Core at 4096, distribution at 8192, access at 61440 with Root Guard on distribution uplinks, rationale (traffic flows toward root making core placement optimal), and PVST+ load balancing are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Only configuring the core switch leaves distribution and access switches at default priority creating risk that a distribution switch (8192 vs 32768) might become root before the core but an access switch with very low MAC could also compete.
explanations.d: STP uses lowest priority number as highest priority; 61440 for core (highest priority number = lowest STP priority) is backward; core should have lowest number (4096).

questions.text: What are the four STP port roles and what does each role mean for how the port handles traffic?

answers.text: STP defines four port roles that determine each port's function in the spanning tree topology: Root Port — the port on a non-root switch that has the best path (lowest cost) to the root bridge; every non-root switch has exactly one root port per VLAN; root ports are always in forwarding state; Designated Port — on each network segment (link between switches) one port is the designated forwarder; the designated switch for a segment is the one closest (lowest cost) to the root bridge; designated ports are always in forwarding state; Alternate Port — a port that has a path to the root bridge but is not the root port; it is kept in blocking state as a backup path; if the root port fails the alternate port can become the new root port; Backup Port — a backup to a designated port on the same segment (occurs when two ports on the same switch connect to the same shared segment); rarely seen in modern point-to-point Ethernet networks; in RSTP (Rapid STP) alternate and backup ports are in discarding state and can rapidly transition if needed; only root ports and designated ports actively forward traffic
STP has two port roles: active and inactive; active ports forward traffic and inactive ports do not; all ports are either active or inactive based on administrator configuration
STP port roles are identical to port states; both terms describe whether a port is currently forwarding traffic or not; root port means forwarding and alternate port means blocking
All ports on the root bridge are designated ports and all ports on non-root switches are root ports; the root bridge has no root ports and non-root switches have no designated ports

explanations.a: All four STP port roles with their definitions (root port as best path to root, designated port as segment forwarder, alternate as backup root path, backup as backup designated path), forwarding/blocking implications, and RSTP discarding state are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: STP has four specific roles (root, designated, alternate, backup) not a simple active/inactive binary; these roles are determined algorithmically not by administrator configuration.
explanations.c: Port roles and port states are distinct concepts; root port is a role that a port plays while forwarding/blocking/learning are states; a root port is in forwarding state but role and state are separate classifications.
explanations.d: The root bridge has all designated ports (it is the closest to itself for all segments) and no root port (root bridge needs no path to itself); non-root switches have exactly one root port and may have designated ports on segments where they are the closest switch to root.

questions.text: Switch A and Switch B are connected with two links. Link 1 (GigabitEthernet0/1) is 1G and Link 2 (GigabitEthernet0/2) is 100M. Switch A is root bridge. What port roles does each port on Switch B have and why?

answers.text: Both ports on Switch B are root ports because Switch B is connected to the root bridge through both links
The 100M port on Switch B becomes the root port because slower ports have higher priority in STP; higher cost means higher priority for root port selection

Switch B has exactly one root port — the port with the lowest cost path to the root bridge; STP path cost is inversely related to link speed (lower speed = higher cost); the cost for 1G (GigabitEthernet0/1) is 4 and for 100M (GigabitEthernet0/2) is 19 in IEEE standard short path costs (or 20000 for 1G and 200000 for 100M in long path costs); the 1G link (lower cost) is selected as the root port because it represents the best path to the root bridge; GigabitEthernet0/1 on Switch B = Root Port (lowest cost path to root, forwarding); the 100M link (higher cost) cannot also be root port — each switch has exactly one root port per VLAN; GigabitEthernet0/2 on Switch B becomes an Alternate Port (has a path to root but is not the best path, in blocking/discarding state); on Switch A (the root bridge) both ports are Designated Ports because the root bridge is always the designated switch for all directly connected segments; in summary: Switch A GigabitEthernet0/1 = Designated (forwarding); Switch A GigabitEthernet0/2 = Designated (forwarding); Switch B GigabitEthernet0/1 = Root Port (forwarding); Switch B GigabitEthernet0/2 = Alternate Port (blocking)
Switch B has no root port because it is not the root bridge; non-root switches are not eligible to have root ports and instead have only alternate ports

explanations.a: Each non-root switch has exactly one root port; having two root ports would create ambiguity about the path to root.
explanations.b: Lower cost (faster link) wins root port selection; higher cost means higher STP cost not higher priority.
explanations.c: Lowest cost (1G = cost 4) determining root port selection, highest cost (100M = cost 19) becoming alternate port, root bridge having all designated ports, and the complete port role assignment for all four ports are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Every non-root switch has exactly one root port per VLAN; the root port is the port providing the best path to the root bridge.

questions.text: A network engineer 'show spanning-tree vlan 20' output shows a port as 'BKN' (Root inconsistent / Root Guard active) instead of its normal role. Under what circumstances does Root Guard change a port's effective role and how does this interact with normal STP port role calculation?

answers.text: Root Guard permanently changes port roles from designated to alternate; once Root Guard activates the port permanently loses its designated role
Root Guard only affects the root port role; it prevents any port from becoming a root port on root-guard-enabled switches; designated and alternate port roles are unaffected
Root Guard changes designated ports to alternate ports permanently and changes alternate ports to backup ports; it reorganizes port roles based on a security policy rather than STP cost calculation

Root Guard does not change the STP-calculated port role but rather overrides the operational state of a port when a superior BPDU is received; the STP algorithm continues to calculate that the port should be in a designated or alternate role based on BPDUs; however Root Guard intervenes when a superior BPDU arrives — superior means the BPDU's Bridge ID is lower than the current root bridge's Bridge ID (indicating the attached device claims to be a better root); Root Guard places the port in root-inconsistent state which operationally means the port does not forward traffic even if STP calculated it as a designated port; the port's calculated role remains (designated or alternate) but the operational state changes to BKN (broken/root inconsistent); the interaction with normal STP: normally a designated port forwards traffic; with Root Guard active on a port that receives a superior BPDU the port enters root-inconsistent state and stops forwarding; STP does not reconverge to use that superior BPDU — the superior BPDU is effectively rejected; the current root bridge remains root; when superior BPDUs stop arriving the port automatically exits root-inconsistent state and returns to its calculated role and state (designated/forwarding); Root Guard is therefore a protection mechanism that prevents topology changes driven by unexpected superior BPDUs without affecting normal STP operation when no superior BPDUs are present

explanations.a: Root Guard does not permanently change port roles; it temporarily overrides the operational state and automatically recovers when superior BPDUs cease.
explanations.b: Root Guard does not specifically target root port role; it prevents any port from propagating a superior BPDU that would change the root bridge for the VLAN.
explanations.c: Root Guard does not reorganize port roles between designated/alternate/backup; it only affects the operational state of the specific port receiving superior BPDUs.
explanations.d: Root Guard overriding operational state while STP continues calculating roles, superior BPDU as the trigger, root-inconsistent state blocking forwarding despite calculated designated role, rejection of superior BPDU preventing reconvergence, and automatic recovery when BPDUs stop are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: After a link failure between the root bridge and a distribution switch, the distribution switch must select a new root port. What STP process determines which of its remaining ports becomes the new root port?

answers.text: After a link failure the distribution switch becomes the new root bridge; when a switch loses its root port it automatically assumes the root bridge role

When a distribution switch loses its root port (its direct link to the root bridge fails) it must select a new root port from its remaining connections; the selection process: the switch sends inferior BPDUs claiming to be root (since it no longer has a better BPDU from the actual root); neighboring switches that have paths to the root bridge respond with superior BPDUs advertising a path to the root; the distribution switch compares the received BPDUs using the STP path cost calculation: root path cost (total accumulated cost from the advertising switch to the root), then sender Bridge ID (tiebreaker if costs are equal), then port ID (tiebreaker if Bridge IDs are equal); the port that receives the BPDU advertising the lowest total root path cost becomes the new root port; in classic STP this convergence takes 30-50 seconds (max age 20 seconds + forward delay 15 seconds x 2); in RSTP the convergence is much faster (1-2 seconds) because alternate ports can transition using the proposal/agreement mechanism without waiting for timers; the port that was previously an alternate port (already had a calculated backup path to root) typically becomes the new root port because the alternate port selection was based on path cost analysis
The switch uses round-robin selection to choose the next root port; ports are assigned root port role in rotation so the port with the next sequential interface number becomes root port
The network administrator must manually designate the new root port using a CLI command; STP cannot automatically select a new root port after the original is lost

explanations.a: Losing the root port does not make the switch become root bridge; the switch sends inferior BPDUs temporarily while reconverging but the actual root bridge remains root.
explanations.b: BPDU exchange to find path to root, path cost comparison tiebreaker sequence (root cost, Bridge ID, port ID), alternate port transitioning to root port, and RSTP vs classic STP convergence time difference are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: STP uses path cost calculation not round-robin selection for port role assignment.
explanations.d: STP automatically selects the new root port through BPDU exchange and cost calculation; manual administrator selection is not required or supported.

questions.text: What are the five STP port states in classic 802.1D STP and what does a port do in each state?

answers.text: Classic 802.1D STP defines five port states that a port transitions through when establishing: Blocking — the port does not forward user data frames and does not learn MAC addresses; it only receives and processes STP BPDUs to determine topology; this prevents loops while STP calculates the topology; Listening — the port participates in STP BPDU exchange to elect root bridge and determine port roles; still does not forward user frames or learn MAC addresses; lasts 15 seconds (forward delay timer); Learning — the port begins populating its MAC address table from received frames but still does not forward user data frames; this prevents flooding when the port transitions to forwarding; lasts 15 seconds (forward delay timer); Forwarding — the port both learns MAC addresses and forwards user data frames normally; only root ports and designated ports reach forwarding state; Disabled — the port is administratively shut down and does not participate in STP or forward any traffic; total transition time from blocking to forwarding is 30 seconds (listening 15s + learning 15s) explaining why users experience a 30-second delay when connecting a device to a switch without PortFast
Classic STP has two port states: forwarding and blocking; a port either forwards all traffic or blocks all traffic with no intermediate states
STP port states are the same as STP port roles; blocking and forwarding are states and alternate and designated are alternative terms for the same two states
Classic STP has three states: learning, forwarding, and discarding; discarding replaces blocking and was introduced in the original 802.1D standard

explanations.a: All five states (blocking, listening, learning, forwarding, disabled) with what each does (BPDU processing, MAC learning, data forwarding), timer durations, and the resulting 30-second delay for non-PortFast ports are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Classic STP has five states including intermediate listening and learning states that provide MAC address stabilization before forwarding begins.
explanations.c: Port states and port roles are distinct STP concepts; blocking is a state and alternate is a role; they are related (alternate ports are in blocking state) but not identical terms.
explanations.d: Discarding is an RSTP state that was introduced in IEEE 802.1w (RSTP) not in the original 802.1D; classic STP uses blocking not discarding.

questions.text: An engineer configures PortFast on a server-facing switch port to eliminate the 30-second STP delay. The server team reports that after the server is rebooted the switch shows the port in forwarding state almost instantly but the server still cannot communicate for about 15 seconds. What causes the residual delay if the STP delay is eliminated?

answers.text: PortFast did not work correctly; the 15-second delay indicates the port is still going through the STP learning state despite PortFast being configured
The 15-second delay is caused by PortFast using a slower learning algorithm than standard STP; PortFast trades speed for accuracy resulting in a 15-second learning phase instead of 30 seconds

PortFast eliminates the STP listening and learning state delays causing the port to immediately transition to forwarding state; however the 15-second residual delay is caused by factors in the server's own network stack initialization rather than STP: network interface initialization time — the server NIC and its driver may take several seconds to fully initialize and assert link; DHCP discovery — if the server uses DHCP it must complete the DHCP four-way handshake (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge) before having a usable IP address; the DHCP process typically takes 2-5 seconds but can take longer if the first request is missed; TCP/IP stack initialization — the server OS network stack requires time to initialize routing tables, start network services, and register the interface; NIC team or bonding negotiation — if the server uses NIC teaming (LACP bonding) the bonding protocol negotiation takes additional time; link aggregation negotiation — the 15 seconds may be LACP negotiation time for the bonded NICs; gratuitous ARP — the server sends gratuitous ARP to populate neighbor caches after obtaining its IP; these delays are in the server's software and hardware not in STP; PortFast correctly eliminated the switch-side delay; the 15-second server-side delay may require server configuration changes (static IP eliminates DHCP wait, NIC bonding optimization)
The 15-second delay is a security feature that cannot be eliminated; switches always enforce a 15-second security quarantine period before allowing new servers to communicate

explanations.a: PortFast is working correctly; the immediate switch port forwarding confirms PortFast eliminated STP delay; the 15-second delay is in the server not the switch.
explanations.b: PortFast does not use a slower learning algorithm; it eliminates the listening and learning states entirely for the initial transition; the port goes directly to forwarding.
explanations.c: PortFast correctly eliminating STP delay and the residual 15 seconds being server-side (NIC initialization, DHCP, stack init, bonding negotiation) with specific resolution suggestions (static IP, bonding optimization) is the correct analysis. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: No mandatory 15-second security quarantine exists in standard STP implementation; the 15-second delay is a server-side operational characteristic.

questions.text: RSTP (802.1w) reduces the five 802.1D STP states to three. What are the RSTP states and how does RSTP achieve rapid convergence without the 30-second delay that blocking-to-forwarding takes in 802.1D?

answers.text: RSTP reduces convergence time by eliminating blocking entirely; all ports in RSTP immediately start in forwarding state and STP calculations happen after forwarding begins
RSTP is faster than 802.1D only when all switches are from the same vendor; mixed-vendor RSTP deployments fall back to 802.1D timers eliminating the speed advantage
RSTP achieves rapid convergence by eliminating BPDU exchange entirely; RSTP uses direct hardware signaling between switches that is faster than BPDU processing

RSTP consolidates 802.1D's five states into three: Discarding — combines the blocking and listening states into one; the port does not forward user frames or learn MAC addresses; it processes BPDUs; designated and alternate ports begin in this state; Learning — same as 802.1D learning; the port learns MAC addresses but does not forward user frames; Forwarding — same as 802.1D forwarding; the port both learns and forwards; RSTP achieves rapid convergence through several mechanisms rather than relying on timer expiration: proposal/agreement handshake — when a designated port wants to move to forwarding it sends a proposal BPDU to the downstream switch; the downstream switch responds with an agreement; this explicit handshake replaces the 802.1D timer-based convergence allowing transitions in milliseconds instead of 30 seconds; edge ports (PortFast equivalent) — ports connected to end devices go directly to forwarding without any delay because they cannot create loops; Alternate and Backup port roles — RSTP pre-calculates alternate paths and holds them in discarding state; when the active path fails the alternate port can immediately transition to forwarding without waiting for timers because STP already knows the alternate path is loop-free; BPDU aging — RSTP detects loss of connectivity faster (3x hello timer = 6 seconds) vs 802.1D's max age timer (20 seconds) allowing faster failure detection

explanations.a: RSTP does not eliminate blocking/discarding; discarding ports still do not forward traffic; convergence speed comes from the proposal/agreement mechanism not immediate forwarding.
explanations.b: RSTP is an IEEE standard (802.1w) that is interoperable between vendors; compatibility with 802.1D is maintained per port (RSTP falls back to 802.1D on individual ports connected to 802.1D-only switches).
explanations.c: RSTP still uses BPDUs; it sends BPDUs every hello interval and uses them for the proposal/agreement mechanism; hardware signaling is not used.
explanations.d: Three RSTP states (discarding, learning, forwarding) with their compositions, and the rapid convergence mechanisms (proposal/agreement handshake, edge ports, pre-calculated alternate paths, faster BPDU aging) are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A switch port is in STP Learning state. What can and cannot happen during this state and why does the Learning state exist before Forwarding?

answers.text: The Learning state is identical to the Forwarding state; the only difference is the name used in show spanning-tree output

During the STP Learning state: what CAN happen — the port processes received frames to populate the MAC address table (it reads the source MAC address of each received frame and records which port it arrived on); the switch can build an accurate MAC address table before forwarding begins; what CANNOT happen — user data frames are not forwarded out the port even though they are processed for MAC learning; the Learning state exists for a critical reason: if the port immediately transitioned from blocking to forwarding without a learning phase the switch would not know the MAC addresses of devices reachable through that port; any unicast frame destined for those devices would be flooded out all ports (unknown unicast flooding) until the MAC address was learned through traffic; in a network with thousands of devices this flooding period would cause significant performance degradation and could overwhelm switches; by spending 15 seconds in Learning state before Forwarding the switch pre-populates its MAC table so that when Forwarding begins it can immediately make forwarding decisions for most destinations without flooding; the 15-second Learning duration is the forward delay timer which is the same duration as the Listening state; total blocking-to-forwarding time is 30 seconds: 15 seconds Listening + 15 seconds Learning
During the Learning state the port forwards all traffic but does not process STP BPDUs; it is a partial forwarding state used during STP convergence to maintain user connectivity
The Learning state only exists on ports that will become root ports; designated ports skip the Learning state and go directly from Listening to Forwarding

explanations.a: Learning and Forwarding are distinct states; Learning populates the MAC table without forwarding user frames while Forwarding does both.
explanations.b: Learning state MAC table population without user frame forwarding, the purpose (pre-populating MAC table to prevent flooding storm when Forwarding begins), and the 15-second duration as part of the 30-second total transition are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: The Learning state does not forward user traffic; it specifically learns MAC addresses while withholding forwarding to prevent loops during convergence.
explanations.d: All ports transitioning from blocking to forwarding pass through both Listening and Learning states regardless of their final role (root or designated).

questions.text: A user's workstation was physically moved to a new desk but is now unable to access network resources that it previously accessed. No IP or software changes were made. What is the most likely cause and how is it confirmed?

answers.text: The most likely cause is that the new desk's switch port is assigned to a different VLAN than the old desk's port; when a workstation is moved physically the network port at the new desk may be in any VLAN — if the old desk was in VLAN 10 (Engineering) and the new desk's port is in VLAN 20 (Finance) the workstation will receive a DHCP address from VLAN 20's scope (different subnet) or fail DHCP entirely if the port is in an unused VLAN; the user cannot access Engineering resources because they are now logically in a different network segment; confirmation: check 'show interfaces switchport' on the new switch port to display the access VLAN assignment; compare it to the correct VLAN for the user's group; alternatively check the IP address the workstation received (if it received an APIPA 169.254.x.x address the VLAN may not have a DHCP server or scope); fix: change the switch port access VLAN to the correct VLAN using 'switchport access vlan [correct-vlan]'
Moving a workstation to a new desk always requires a new IP address; the user must request a new IP address from IT before connectivity can be restored after a desk move
Moving a workstation is always seamless if no cables were changed; since no physical cable changes were made the problem must be in the workstation's software configuration
The issue is that the switch does not support the user's VLAN at the new desk location; add the user's VLAN to the switch's VLAN database before assigning the port

explanations.a: Physical move to a different VLAN causing wrong subnet assignment (different DHCP scope or APIPA), confirmation via 'show interfaces switchport', and 'switchport access vlan' fix are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Moving to a new desk does not inherently require a new IP; if the new port is in the same VLAN the workstation receives its address normally; the issue is VLAN assignment not IP management policy.
explanations.c: Moving to a new desk changes the physical port which has its own VLAN configuration; the problem is in the switch port configuration not the workstation software.
explanations.d: The VLAN likely exists on the switch already; the issue is the specific port not being assigned to the correct VLAN, not the VLAN being absent from the switch.

questions.text: A switch trunk port between two switches shows VLAN 100 as inactive even though VLAN 100 devices exist on both switches. 'show interfaces trunk' shows VLAN 100 is in the allowed list but not in the active list. What causes VLAN 100 to be inactive on the trunk and how is it resolved?

answers.text: VLANs on trunk ports are always active if they appear in the allowed list; inactive status always indicates a hardware failure in the trunk link itself
VLAN 100 inactive on the trunk means there are no active ports in VLAN 100 on either switch; add at least one access port to VLAN 100 on each switch to activate it on the trunk

A VLAN appearing in the allowed list but not the active list on a trunk indicates the VLAN is not active on that specific switch; common causes: VLAN 100 does not exist in the local VLAN database — the VLAN must be created on both switches with 'vlan 100' in global configuration mode; on Cisco Catalyst switches a VLAN that is allowed on a trunk but not in the local VLAN database shows as inactive; the VLAN was pruned by VTP — if VTP is configured and the switch receives a VTP update that removes VLAN 100 or the VTP domain mismatch causes the switch not to learn VLAN 100 it becomes inactive on the trunk; the VLAN is in the allowed list but is being STP blocked — check if STP has placed VLAN 100's trunk interface in blocking state for that VLAN; the VLAN is suspended — the VLAN state is suspended rather than active (show vlan id 100 shows state); VTP mode mismatch — if one switch is in VTP transparent mode it does not propagate VLAN database updates from VTP servers; resolution: verify VLAN 100 exists on both switches with 'show vlan id 100'; if absent create it with 'vlan 100' and verify it shows active in 'show vlan brief'; verify VTP domain and mode consistency between both switches

The inactive status is normal for VLANs that have not recently carried traffic; VLANs only show as active when they have frames being forwarded; wait for VLAN 100 traffic to appear and it will automatically become active

explanations.a: VLAN inactivity on a trunk is a configuration state not a hardware failure; the allowed list and active list are distinct concepts.
explanations.b: VLAN 100 becomes active on the trunk when it exists in the VLAN database regardless of whether access ports are assigned to it; having active access ports helps but the VLAN database entry is the primary requirement.
explanations.c: VLAN not existing in local database (most common cause), VTP pruning, STP blocking, VLAN suspended state, and VTP mode mismatch as causes with 'show vlan id' verification and 'vlan 100' creation as resolution are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: VLAN active/inactive on a trunk is a configuration state not a traffic-dependent state; a VLAN in the database shows active whether or not it currently carries traffic.

questions.text: A VoIP deployment uses a voice VLAN (VLAN 30) and a data VLAN (VLAN 10) on the same switch ports. Phones should tag their traffic as VLAN 30 and PCs connected through the phone should use VLAN 10. After configuration, phone calls work but PC network access is intermittent. 'show interfaces switchport' shows: access VLAN 10, voice VLAN 30, trunking mode: access. What configuration issue might cause intermittent PC access?

answers.text: The configuration is correct; intermittent PC access is always caused by PC hardware issues when voice VLANs are configured correctly
Trunking mode 'access' is incorrect for voice VLAN ports; change the port to trunk mode to support both VLAN 10 and VLAN 30 simultaneously; access mode ports only support one VLAN

The access VLAN and voice VLAN cannot coexist on the same physical port; phones and PCs must be on separate physical switch ports; the solution is running a separate cable directly from the PC to the switch bypassing the phone

The configuration appears correct at first glance (access VLAN 10 for data, voice VLAN 30 for phone) but several subtle issues can cause intermittent PC access in a voice VLAN deployment: CDP/LLDP-MED signaling — the switch uses CDP or LLDP-MED to tell the phone which VLAN to use for voice; if CDP is disabled on the port or the phone does not support it the phone may not tag traffic as VLAN 30 and instead sends voice traffic untagged which competes with PC traffic on the access VLAN (VLAN 10); STP state on the phone's PC-facing port — Cisco phones have an internal switch; if the phone's internal switch port is experiencing STP issues (not common but possible with some phone models) PC connectivity is intermittent; PoE power issues — if the phone is not receiving stable PoE power the phone's internal switch resets intermittently dropping the PC connection each time; the phone connects to its PC port after fully booting; QoS misconfiguration — if CoS markings from the PC are being incorrectly trusted and placed in the voice queue the data traffic may be incorrectly prioritized causing competing traffic to be dropped; most likely cause for intermittent rather than complete failure: the phone may be briefly resetting its internal switch due to PoE instability causing the PC port to momentarily lose connectivity; check 'show power inline' for any power faults on the phone port and verify stable PoE delivery

explanations.a: Intermittent PC access in a voice VLAN deployment has network-side causes worth investigating before assuming PC hardware failure.
explanations.b: Cisco switch ports configured for voice VLAN use a special mode that is shown as 'access' mode in show interfaces switchport but actually handles the untagged access VLAN for PC and the tagged voice VLAN for the phone; changing to full trunk mode would break the standard voice VLAN configuration.
explanations.c: Access + voice VLAN coexistence on one port is a designed Cisco feature; the phone handles tagging while the PC uses the untagged access VLAN; separate ports are not required.
explanations.d: CDP/LLDP-MED signaling for voice VLAN, phone internal switch behavior, PoE instability causing phone resets affecting the PC port, and QoS misconfiguration as possible causes with PoE stability check as the most likely cause for intermittent (not complete) failure are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: Two switches use VTP to synchronize VLAN databases. An administrator accidentally adds a switch with a higher VTP revision number in transparent mode and then changes it to server mode. All VLANs are wiped from both switches. What happened and what should have prevented this?

answers.text: VTP transparent mode switches always reset VLAN databases when changed to server mode; this is expected behavior and the VLANs must be manually recreated

This is the classic VTP revision number attack or accidental VTP overwrite: when a new switch is added in transparent mode it maintains its own VLAN database but does not propagate VTP updates; however it may have been previously configured with a high revision number (revision numbers increment each time VLANs are added or removed); when the switch was changed to server mode it began sending VTP advertisements with its high revision number; VTP domains always accept updates from the switch with the highest revision number as it is assumed to have the most current information; if the new switch's revision number exceeded the production switches' revision number (even with a minimal or empty VLAN database) the production switches replaced their entire VLAN database with the new switch's empty database; all production VLANs were deleted because the higher revision number was interpreted as authoritative; prevention: before adding any switch to a VTP domain reset its revision number to 0 by either changing the switch to VTP transparent mode and back to server/client mode (this resets revision number) or changing the VTP domain name to a non-existent domain and then back (also resets revision); use VTP version 3 which has a primary server concept that requires explicit designation of the authoritative server preventing rogue high-revision updates; implement change control procedures requiring revision number verification before adding new switches; consider using VTP transparent mode throughout which avoids VTP altogether while still allowing manual VLAN consistency
VTP only affects VLAN names not VLAN existence; the VLANs were not actually deleted but their names were changed; run 'show vlan brief' to see the VLANs with their new names

The problem was caused by adding the switch in transparent mode first; switches should always be added directly in server or client mode; transparent mode is incompatible with VTP and causes database corruption

explanations.a: VTP transparent to server mode change does not automatically wipe other switches' databases; the high revision number is the specific mechanism causing the overwrite.
explanations.b: High revision number causing authoritative overwrite of production VLAN database, revision number reset procedure (transparent mode toggle or domain name change), VTP version 3 primary server as the architectural fix, and transparent mode as the safe alternative are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: VTP updates change the entire VLAN database including VLAN existence not just names; VLANs not in the VTP update are deleted from all client switches.
explanations.d: Adding in transparent mode first is actually safer than adding directly as server/client because transparent mode does not receive or send VTP updates; the danger was changing the mode to server after having a high revision number.

questions.text: A network engineer suspects an ACL is blocking traffic between two subnets. What commands help identify which ACL is causing the block and what information do they provide?

answers.text: Several commands help identify ACL blocking issues: 'show ip access-lists' — displays all configured IP ACLs with their rules and crucially the hit counts for each rule; a deny rule with an incrementing hit count confirms it is actively matching and discarding traffic; zero hits on a permit rule that should match traffic suggests the traffic is being denied by an earlier rule; 'show ip interface [interface]' — shows which ACL is applied to each interface including the direction (inbound or outbound); this identifies which interface has ACL enforcement and in which direction; 'show running-config | include ip access' or 'show running-config interface [interface]' — shows the ACL binding to interfaces in the full configuration; 'show access-lists' — shows all access lists including named and numbered; 'ping' and 'traceroute' — if ping from the source subnet fails but traceroute shows packets reaching the router that connects the subnets the ACL is likely on the router interface; the diagnostic approach is: identify which router/firewall the traffic passes through, check what ACLs are applied to that interface, review the ACL rules with hit counts, and identify the specific deny rule with a high hit count that corresponds to the blocked traffic
ACL troubleshooting requires a protocol analyzer; show commands cannot identify ACL blocking because ACLs operate at hardware level and are not visible through software commands

The only way to identify an ACL block is to remove all ACLs and test connectivity; if connectivity restores after removal ACLs were the cause; then reapply ACLs one by one until the blocking rule is found
ACL hit counts only increment once per session; a deny rule with a hit count of 1 is actively blocking all traffic in that session permanently

explanations.a: Show ip access-lists (with hit counts identifying active deny rules), show ip interface (identifying which interfaces have ACLs in which direction), and the diagnostic workflow (identify router, check interface ACL, review hit counts) are all correct. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Show commands directly expose ACL configuration and hit counts; ACL troubleshooting without show commands would be unnecessarily difficult.
explanations.c: Removing all ACLs is disruptive and unnecessary; show commands identify the specific blocking rule without removing security controls.
explanations.d: ACL hit counts increment for every matching packet not once per session; a deny rule with thousands of hits is actively discarding thousands of individual packets.

questions.text: An ACL is designed to permit HTTP (port 80) and HTTPS (port 443) traffic from 192.168.1.0/24 to any destination while blocking all other traffic. After applying the ACL, users can browse HTTPS websites but HTTP fails. The ACL has: Permit TCP 192.168.1.0/24 any eq 443, Permit TCP 192.168.1.0/24 any eq 80, Deny IP any any. Why does HTTP fail and what is the corrected ACL?

answers.text: HTTP fails because port 80 traffic is blocked by default on all routers regardless of ACL configuration; HTTP requires a special router license to permit
The ACL is correct but applied to the wrong interface; move the ACL from the outbound interface to the inbound interface and both HTTP and HTTPS will work

The issue is likely the ACL direction or the return traffic: if this ACL is applied INBOUND on the router interface facing users it permits user-initiated HTTP and HTTPS traffic going outbound to the internet; however the ACL also affects RETURN traffic — when the web server responds to an HTTP request the response comes back as TCP traffic from a destination port in the ephemeral range to source port 80 on the user's side; the response traffic may not match the permit statement which specifies 'eq 80' as the destination port; the return traffic has 80 as the SOURCE port not destination; if applied INBOUND on the internet-facing interface instead the ACL would block response traffic on both HTTP and HTTPS; troubleshooting approach: check which interface the ACL is applied to and in which direction; if it is applied outbound on the LAN interface both HTTP and HTTPS should work (user-initiated traffic matches the permit rules); if HTTPS works but HTTP does not check if there is a separate security policy blocking HTTP (content filtering, URL filtering); check ACL hit counts for both permit rules — if the HTTPS rule has hits but HTTP rule has zero hits the HTTP traffic is not reaching the ACL meaning it may be blocked before that point; verify with 'show ip access-lists' that both rules have incremented matches when HTTP is attempted; an HTTPS-only scenario might also result from browser security policies not the ACL
The permit statement for HTTP must use 'eq http' instead of 'eq 80'; Cisco ACLs use service names not port numbers and 'eq 80' is not recognized

explanations.a: HTTP does not require a special router license; port 80 is a standard well-known port that routers permit through ACLs without special configuration.
explanations.b: Simply moving the ACL without understanding the traffic direction may create different problems; the diagnosis requires understanding specifically why HTTPS works but HTTP does not.
explanations.c: Return traffic direction analysis, ACL direction (inbound vs outbound) determining which traffic is filtered, hit count verification to confirm which permit rule is matching HTTP traffic, and browser security policy as an alternative explanation are the correct diagnostic considerations. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Cisco IOS ACLs accept both service names (eq http) and port numbers (eq 80) interchangeably; 'eq 80' is valid syntax.

questions.text: A firewall ACL is expected to block only SSH (port 22) from external sources while allowing all other traffic. After deployment, SSH from external is blocked as intended, but internal users report that SFTP (which uses port 22) to the company file server also stopped working. Why and how should the ACL be rewritten to fix both requirements?

answers.text: SFTP uses a different port than SSH; SFTP uses port 21 and the ACL should permit port 21 explicitly; port 22 blocking does not affect SFTP

Internal users should not be able to use SFTP to external servers through a firewall; the correct policy is to block all port 22 traffic from both internal and external sources and use an internal SFTP server instead
Configure SFTP on port 2222 instead of port 22; SFTP can use any configured port and moving it to 2222 allows the port 22 ACL to block SSH without affecting SFTP

SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) runs over SSH on TCP port 22; it is not FTP on port 21 — SFTP is a completely different protocol that uses SSH as its transport layer; when the ACL blocks TCP port 22 from external sources it also blocks SFTP because both use the same port; if the ACL is: Deny TCP any any eq 22; Permit IP any any — applied to the internet-facing interface inbound — it blocks all port 22 traffic including internal users' SFTP connections that exit through the firewall and loop back (or go to a server in the DMZ); the fix requires making the deny rule source-specific rather than any-to-any: Deny TCP [external IP ranges or NOT internal ranges] any eq 22 should be replaced with more specific source matching; correct approach: identify the trusted source ranges for SSH access (management networks, specific admin IP addresses) and permit only those while denying all others; example refined ACL: Permit TCP [admin-network] any eq 22 (allow admin SSH), Permit TCP [internal-network] any eq 22 (allow internal SFTP), Deny TCP any any eq 22 (block external SSH), Permit IP any any; placing the permit rules for trusted sources before the broad deny ensures internal SFTP is permitted while external SSH is blocked; also consider whether SSH from external should be completely blocked or only from unknown IPs (a VPN-only SSH policy achieves better security than port blocking alone)

explanations.a: SFTP uses port 22 (SSH), not port 21 which is FTP control port; this is a common confusion — SFTP and FTP are completely different protocols.
explanations.b: Internal users having SFTP access to external servers is a legitimate business requirement; a blanket block of all port 22 traffic is overly restrictive.
explanations.c: While SFTP can be configured on non-standard ports this requires server and client configuration changes; the correct fix is ACL refinement not service reconfiguration.
explanations.d: SFTP running on TCP 22 over SSH being blocked by the same rule, source-specific ACL rules permitting internal and admin traffic while denying external SSH, and the refined ACL structure with specific permits before broad deny are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network engineer applies an ACL to restrict traffic but accidentally creates an implicit allow instead of an implicit deny. The ACL ends with 'Permit IP any any' as the last rule. Users report they can access resources that should be blocked. How does this error occur and what is the implicit deny rule that all properly written ACLs should rely on?

answers.text: There is no implicit deny in any ACL; all ACLs must explicitly deny every unwanted traffic type; an ACL with no deny statements permits all traffic

This error occurs when an administrator adds 'Permit IP any any' as a catch-all rule at the end of an ACL intending it as a fallback or troubleshooting aid but forgetting to remove it before applying to production; the permit any any rule matches every packet that was not matched by earlier rules and permits it — creating an implicit allow for all unmatched traffic; the correct behavior relies on the implicit deny: every Cisco IOS ACL (and most firewall ACL implementations) has an invisible implicit 'Deny IP any any' as the very last rule; if a packet does not match any explicit permit or deny rule in the ACL it is matched by this implicit deny and discarded; this is the fundamental security principle of deny-by-default — traffic not explicitly permitted is denied; an administrator should never add an explicit 'permit ip any any' unless they truly intend to allow all traffic that was not explicitly denied earlier in the ACL; to fix the current ACL: remove the 'permit ip any any' rule at the end and rely on the implicit deny; verify the removal with 'show ip access-lists' and confirm that traffic that should be blocked is now denied; the implicit deny is not visible in 'show ip access-lists' output but it is always present and always enforced
The implicit deny in Cisco ACLs only applies to TCP traffic; UDP and ICMP traffic requires explicit deny rules; add explicit deny statements for UDP and ICMP to fix the partial allow condition
